summary: Gruber argues that WALL-E was the best picture of 2008 but was effectively barred from competing for the top Oscar by the existence of the 'Best Animated Feature' category. He contends that animation is merely a technique, not a lesser form of cinema, and that segregating animated films into their own category is dismissive. Drawing parallels to historic Oscar snubs like Citizen Kane losing to How Green Was My Valley, he predicts WALL-E will outlast the Academy's actual choices in cultural memory. He frames the animated feature category as a ghetto that prevents great films from competing on equal footing.
key_points: The Best Animated Feature category functions as a ghetto that prevents animated films from serious Best Picture consideration
Animation is a technique, not a genre — cinema is cinema regardless of how it's produced
WALL-E was the best film of 2008 but couldn't realistically compete for Best Picture due to institutional bias against animation
The Academy has a long history of choosing inferior films for Best Picture, as time repeatedly reveals
Great overlooked films like WALL-E and 2001: A Space Odyssey will be remembered long after the Academy's choices are forgotten
money_quotes: I watch the Oscars every year because I love movies. I hate the Oscars because I love movies.
But why does this category even exist? Animated as opposed to what? Photographed? Animation is merely a technique. Cinema is cinema.
The whole point of this award is to establish a ghetto where "cartoons", no matter how good, are relegated.

Putting WALL-E up against Bolt and Kung Fu Panda rather than letting it compete against Slumdog Millionaire and Benjamin Button is like requiring a 13-year-old chess prodigy to compete only against other children, regardless of whether he could stand his own against adult grandmasters. It's a dismissive pat on the head.
That WALL-E is for children does not mean it is only for children. It is a precise, painstakingly detailed, evocative work of art that rewards multiple viewings.

WALL-E, it says here, will win out in the end: not just remembered but still being watched, loved, and critically acclaimed generations after it was released, and long after the Academy's choices have been forgotten.
key_insight: The Academy's Best Animated Feature category is a patronizing ghetto that prevents animated masterpieces from competing as what they are — cinema.
